frameworks.name: reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: agents learn action-values to maximize cumulative reward over tasks

frameworks.name: rate-distortion
frameworks.one_line: memory precision is costly, traded off against expected-reward loss

frameworks.name: resource-rational analysis
frameworks.one_line: bounded agents allocate limited capacity to high-impact representations

core_question: How should an agent with limited memory precision allocate that precision across action-value representations to maximize expected reward?
key_assumptions: memory precision of each action-value is costly and adjustable
biological and artificial agents face shared capacity constraints
action-values vary in their impact on cumulative reward
manipulations: precision (resource budget) allocated to each remembered action-value
initial resource budget at the start of learning
task type (two standard RL tasks plus a model-based planning task)
key_findings: DRA assigns more resources to memories with higher impact on cumulative rewards
starting with a higher resource budget yields faster learning than the eventual asymptotic budget
algorithm derived from first principles applies across standard and model-based RL tasks
interpretations: differential precision allocation provides a normative account of bounded-rational learning under cost
early-learning over-engagement of frontal cortex may reflect transiently higher resource budgets
